meta.name: CORE-10 Clinical Outcomes in Routine Evaluation
meta.description: The CORE-10 is a brief 10-item self-report scale that assesses the overall level of psychological distress. It covers anxiety, depression, trauma, physical functioning, social functioning, and risk, widely used in routine outcome monitoring in clinical practice.
meta.author: Connell & Barkham (2007)
meta.reference: Connell J, Barkham M. CORE-10 User Manual, Version 1.1. CORE System Trust, 2007.
meta.instruction: Please select the option that best describes how you have been over the last week.

commonChoices.label: Most or all of the time

questions.text: I have felt tense, anxious, or nervous
I have felt I have someone to turn to for support when needed
I have felt unable to cope when things go wrong
Talking to people, walking, or getting dressed has been slower than usual
I have lost interest in life
I have been angry at myself
I have felt OK about myself physically and have had enough energy
I have not wanted to be with people
I have felt low or unhappy
Unwanted thoughts of ending my life have come into my mind

ranges.label: Normal
ranges.description: Your psychological distress level is within the normal range, suggesting you are coping well with everyday challenges. Most people in the general population score here. Continue nurturing habits and relationships that support your well-being.
suggestions: Continue maintaining the healthy habits and routines that support your well-being
Stay socially connected and engaged in activities that bring you meaning and enjoyment
Be aware that mental health can fluctuate, and seeking support early during difficult periods is beneficial

ranges.label: Mild Distress
ranges.description: You are experiencing mild psychological distress that may be affecting your mood, energy, or daily functioning. This is common during stressful life periods and often responds to self-care. If symptoms persist, consider professional consultation.
suggestions: Pay attention to emotional changes and identify the specific stressors contributing to your distress
Practice evidence-based stress management techniques such as mindfulness, progressive muscle relaxation, or deep breathing
Maintain regular physical exercise, which has strong evidence for improving mood and reducing anxiety
Consider seeking professional help if your distress persists or begins to interfere with daily responsibilities

ranges.label: Moderate Distress
ranges.description: Your psychological distress is elevated and likely affecting work, relationships, and daily functioning. This score falls in the clinical range seen in people seeking mental health services. Professional support can help develop coping strategies.
suggestions: Seeking counseling or psychotherapy is recommended at this level of distress
Share your feelings with a trusted friend, family member, or colleague for emotional support
Maintain regular routines including consistent sleep, meals, and physical activity
Avoid relying on alcohol, substances, or other unhealthy coping mechanisms to manage your distress
Consider whether workplace or relationship issues may be contributing factors that need to be addressed

ranges.label: Severe Distress
ranges.description: Your psychological distress is very high and likely impairing daily functioning, work, and relationships. This level of distress typically warrants prompt professional intervention. Please prioritize seeking help, as effective treatments exist.
suggestions: Please seek professional mental health help as soon as possible; this level of distress is treatable
If you are having thoughts of self-harm or suicide, contact the 988 Suicide and Crisis Lifeline immediately
Ensure you have trusted people around you who can provide support during this difficult time
Seeking help is a sign of strength, not weakness, and effective treatments such as therapy and medication are available
Focus on basic self-care including sleep, nutrition, and safety while you arrange professional support
